title: account.export_refused audit row re-leaks schema reconnaissance through the next successful export's audit_log_for_user projection
summary: The fix for Finding 2 (specialist security) stripped failed_tables/row_capped_tables/parent_id_truncated_tables from the client response body to prevent schema reconnaissance. But the new account.export_refused audit row writes the same lists (row_capped_tables, parent_id_truncated_tables, incomplete_reasons strings containing comma-joined table names) into audit_log.metadata under user_id=subject (because logAuditEvent uses the user-scoped client, log_audit_event derives user_id from auth.uid()). When the subject next produces a successful export, redactAuditLogForUser retains this row (row.user_id === userId branch) AND the metadata is NOT in AUDIT_METADATA_REDACT_KEYS — so the same schema reconnaissance the body-strip blocked is now durably embedded in the user's GDPR bundle. A subject who systematically triggers refusals (by uploading until they hit the 100MB cap, etc.) can read their own bundle to enumerate every internal table name in row_capped_tables / parent_id_truncated_tables / incomplete_reasons. Worse: an admin with audit_log read access can pivot on any user's audit rows to enumerate which users hit which truncation modes — turning the refusal audit into a global schema map. Finding 2's intent was 'detail is server-log-only' but the audit row IS server-and-bundle.
evidence: route.ts:133-145 writes incomplete_reasons + row_capped_tables + parent_id_truncated_tables verbatim into audit_log.metadata via logAuditEvent(supabase, { action: 'account.export_refused', ... }). gdpr-export.ts:192-226 AUDIT_METADATA_REDACT_KEYS has no entry for 'row_capped_tables', 'parent_id_truncated_tables', 'incomplete_reasons', or 'failed_tables'. gdpr-export.ts:286-326 redactAuditLogForUser retains the row (subject is actor — the user-scoped log_audit_event writes user_id=subject) and passes metadata through unredacted.
fix: Either (a) strip the table-name lists from account.export_refused metadata — keep request_id + the truncation booleans only (which are 'this user hit truncation' WITHOUT revealing which tables); or (b) add 'row_capped_tables', 'parent_id_truncated_tables', 'failed_tables', 'incomplete_reasons' to AUDIT_METADATA_REDACT_KEYS so they blank out at export time. Option (a) is the surgical fix because the table-name lists serve no user-facing forensic purpose — support reproduces from request_id + server log.

title: Rate-limit token consumed before refusal gate — user with truncated data is permanently locked out of exports (DoS via permanent-refusal loop)
summary: The order of operations is: csrf → auth → checkLimit (consumes 1/day token) → collectUserExportBundle → refusal gate. A user whose data hits ANY truncation mode (size cap, per-table row cap, parent-id cap) consumes their 1/day token, gets 500 export_truncated, and has no way to retry today. Tomorrow they consume their next token, hit the same deterministic truncation (size of their data didn't shrink), and get refused again. The user is permanently locked out of GDPR Art. 15 fulfilment by their own data volume. The route comment line 80-86 explicitly chose this policy ('user retries on the next rate-limit window') — but it doesn't actually unblock the user, because the rate-limit window doesn't change the bundle's truncation state. The user must contact support. Meanwhile, the route gate refuses BEFORE the storage upload (cheap), but AFTER the bundle assembly (expensive — bounded-concurrency fetch of 26 tables). A scripted attacker can exhaust their own token and trigger 26-table fan-out DoS against Supabase pooler with each daily call. More importantly: any user with > EXPORT_SIZE_CAP_BYTES of legitimate data cannot exercise their GDPR right of access through this endpoint — a regulator-visible Art. 15 violation.
evidence: route.ts:55-64 (checkLimit consumes token), route.ts:71 (collectUserExportBundle is the expensive step), route.ts:116-156 (refusal gate runs AFTER, returns 500). Truncation is deterministic for a given data set — same bundle, same refusal, next day.
fix: On refusal-gate trip, refund the rate-limit token (call exportLimiter's API to credit one back) OR run a cheap pre-flight `select count(*)` against the heaviest tables (trades, audit_log) before token consumption to predict truncation. Alternatively: surface a 'contact support' admin escalation path explicitly in the 500 body and queue a partial-export-with-acknowledgement workflow that bypasses the same-day limit. Minimum surgical fix: refund the token on refusal.

title: Indirect orphan-id row triggers fail-loud → entire export permanently refused for a user with even one historical FK orphan
summary: Chains specialist silent-failure-hunter MED conf-8 (non-string parent PK) with the apply-pass fail-loud added at line 1432. The apply pass added: if parentIds.length < parentIdsRaw.length, return fetch_error 'indirect parent id type mismatch'. The intent was to catch a future bigint PK. But the trigger fires on ANY non-string value in r[parentIdColumn] — INCLUDING a null. If a user's parent table (strategies, portfolios) has even ONE row with id=NULL (legacy data, a buggy seed, a failed migration that left a row with id default removed), the typeof string filter drops that row, the fail-loud returns fetch_error, the bundle's partial gate refuses the entire export (NOT just that one indirect table). Combined with chain #2 above (rate-limit token consumed first), this user is permanently locked out of ALL exports by a single orphan row in a single parent table. No automated remediation; support must locate and fix the orphan. This is a DoS vector via data quality: a buggy migration that left null PKs on 0.001% of rows takes down GDPR for those 0.001% of users.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:1422-1441 — `.filter((v): v is string => typeof v === 'string')` drops null, then `parentIds.length < parentIdsRaw.length` triggers fail-loud. The fail-loud's error message says 'expected string PK' but the actual trigger is null OR non-string OR composite OR bigint — all conflated into one diagnostic.
fix: Split the type-mismatch detection: (a) tolerate nulls (filter them silently with a metric counter — they're legitimate dropped rows, not a manifest bug); (b) fail loud ONLY when the dropped values are non-null non-string (the bigint/composite case the comment actually targets). Pattern: `const nonNullIds = parentIdsRaw.filter(v => v !== null); const stringIds = nonNullIds.filter((v): v is string => typeof v === 'string'); if (stringIds.length < nonNullIds.length) { return fail_loud(...); }`.

title: encodeExportBundle has NO direct unit tests of byte output — round-trip invariant is unverified
summary: The apply pass introduced encodeExportBundle as a hand-rolled streaming JSON serializer (templated concat of envelope fields + per-table wrapper + cached row strings + closer). It is the SINGLE point where the on-the-wire bundle bytes are produced. Yet no test in the suite asserts that the output is valid JSON, that JSON.parse(encodeExportBundle(b)) === b structurally, or that the byte-precise output matches JSON.stringify(b). The route test (gdpr-export-route.test.ts:78-87) MOCKS encodeExportBundle entirely with `new TextEncoder().encode(JSON.stringify(bundle))` — so the route test is a tautology that proves nothing about the real implementation. The only real assertion (line 131-152) is a SOURCE GREP that verifies the route CALLS encodeExportBundle(bundle) — it never verifies the output is correct. Consequence: any of (a) wrong field order, (b) missing comma between adjacent rows, (c) `JSON.stringify(undefined)` returning unquoted 'undefined' for an absent optional field, (d) a re-ordering of the ExportBundle literal in collectUserExportBundle that diverges from the hand-rolled order in encodeExportBundle — would all silently ship a malformed JSON bundle to storage. The downstream consumer (download script, regulator-facing serializer) would see JSON.parse throw. Note: the fallback branch at line 898 (when `cached.length !== t.rows.length`) calls JSON.stringify per row, so length mismatch silently produces a DIFFERENT serialization than the cached path — two callers can produce divergent bytes for the same bundle.
evidence: grep for encodeExportBundle in src/__tests__ shows: only the route test, only mocked. gdpr-export.test.ts has NO `encodeExportBundle(` test. The fallback at gdpr-export.ts:898 vs the cached path at gdpr-export.ts:894 are NEVER differentially tested. The route test mock at gdpr-export-route.test.ts:85-86 explicitly stubs out the function, so 'route uses encodeExportBundle' is a syntactic claim only.
fix: Add a direct unit test: `describe('encodeExportBundle', () => { it('produces JSON that JSON.parse round-trips structurally', () => { const b = makeBundle(); const bytes = encodeExportBundle(b); const text = new TextDecoder().decode(bytes); const parsed = JSON.parse(text); expect(parsed).toEqual(JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(b))); }); it('cached and fallback paths produce identical bytes for the same bundle', ...); it('matches JSON.stringify(bundle) byte-for-byte', ...); })`. Without this, the hand-rolled serializer is a load-bearing component with zero behavioral coverage.

title: ROW_JSON_CACHE staleness on in-place row mutation — length-equal mutation ships pre-mutation JSON
summary: ROW_JSON_CACHE is a module-level WeakMap<ExportTablePayload, readonly string[]>. After collectUserExportBundle returns, the bundle object is passed to encodeExportBundle. The cache lookup checks `cached.length === t.rows.length`. If a caller (or a future post-processing step) MUTATES `t.rows` in place (replacing rows, redacting fields, etc.) WITHOUT changing the array length, encodeExportBundle uses the STALE cached JSON strings — silently shipping the pre-mutation row content to storage. The bundle object's .rows array now diverges from the encoded bytes. This is a subtle invariant: the cache assumes rows is immutable post-collect. Today no caller mutates rows, but: (a) the route MAY (future PR) want to scrub a field at the route layer for some custom retention policy, (b) a test could trivially trigger this by `.rows[0].field = X`, (c) the test suite's mocked encodeExportBundle would never catch the divergence. Worse: the staleness is silent — no error, no warning, no diff between bundle and bytes visible to the route. Even worse for security: a defense-in-depth pattern of 're-redact at the route layer' (which a CSO might recommend after a leak) would be completely defeated by the cache.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:656 WeakMap declaration. gdpr-export.ts:892-896: `const cached = ROW_JSON_CACHE.get(t); if (cached && cached.length === t.rows.length) { for (let r = 0; r < cached.length; r += 1) { chunks.push(SHARED_ENCODER.encode(cached[r])); } }` — uses cached[r] regardless of whether t.rows[r] still matches. The length check is the ONLY freshness signal.
fix: Either (a) deep-freeze the rows after caching (Object.freeze on each row + freeze the array, so mutation throws in strict mode), or (b) drop the cache and inline-streamify the serialization in collectUserExportBundle (single function call produces both bundle + bytes), or (c) hash each cached entry's JSON string and compare against a re-stringify of t.rows[r] before using it (defeats the perf optimization). The cleanest fix is (b): merge encodeExportBundle into collectUserExportBundle as a streaming write, so the bundle is never a mutable intermediate.

title: Top-level row.user_id (admin actor UUID) is NOT redacted on entity/target-retained rows — admin identity still leaks despite the apply-pass metadata fix
summary: Apply-pass extended AUDIT_METADATA_REDACT_KEYS with 13 admin-actor UUID keys (granted_by, approved_by, etc.) to scrub admin identity from metadata. But the redaction operates ONLY on the metadata object — line 320: `clone.metadata = redactMetadataValue(meta);`. The TOP-LEVEL `row.user_id` column (which is the actor's UUID per audit.ts convention — see audit.ts:173 'derives user_id from auth.uid() inside Postgres') is NEVER touched by redactAuditLogForUser. For Finding 7 retention modes (isEntity = subject is entity; isMetaTarget = subject is metadata target), the actor (row.user_id) is by definition ANOTHER user — typically an admin. Specialist security LOW conf-7 #2 flagged this exact pattern PRE-apply; apply pass did not address it; AND the apply pass MADE IT WORSE by adding the entity-based retention modes (more admin-acted-on-subject rows are now retained, each leaking the admin's auth.users UUID via the top-level user_id column). A subject can correlate `row.user_id` across role.grant / deletion.request.approve / account.sanitize / admin.kill_switch rows to enumerate every admin's UUID and identify which admin acted on them. The audit comment explicitly commits to scrubbing 'cross-party identifiers' but the top-level cross-party identifier (admin's user_id) sits in plain sight.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:315 `const clone: Record<string, unknown> = { ...row };` shallow-copies including row.user_id. gdpr-export.ts:319-321 only mutates clone.metadata. The retention reason check (line 306-313) doesn't propagate which mode triggered retention, so the redactor cannot know whether row.user_id is the subject (safe) or an admin (unsafe).
fix: In redactAuditLogForUser, track which retention mode triggered the keep and redact accordingly: `if (!isActor) { clone.user_id = REDACTED_PLACEHOLDER; }` — when the subject is only the entity or metadata target, the actor's id MUST be blanked. Add an integration test that pins this for role.grant + deletion.request.approve + admin.kill_switch rows.

title: rowsForTable and projectedRowsForTable are dead code with no callers — null-return-on-missing contract is unenforced
summary: `grep -rn 'rowsForTable\|projectedRowsForTable' --include='*.ts' --include='*.tsx'` outside gdpr-export.ts and tests returns ZERO matches. The apply pass narrowed both helpers' return type to `Array<...> | null` (silent-failure MED conf-9) — but the helpers are never called from production code. There IS no caller to discriminate `null` from `[]`. The 'force the caller to disambiguate' rationale (commit d3cb3ec7 message) is aspirational: no caller exists. The type narrowing also includes a test for `rowsForTable` (M-0521 pin) but the helper itself is dead. The risk is two-fold: (a) the type contract migrates as a slow drift (a future caller will treat null and [] as identical and the schema-drift safety vanishes); (b) the helpers' bug surface is untested in production wiring — e.g., the cast `entry.rows as Array<PublicRow<T>>` (line 747) is a type assertion that a malformed entry could violate at runtime, but no caller depends on it. Suggested fix: either delete both helpers (dead code; YAGNI) or wire ONE caller (the route's audit_log download path, the regulator-facing serializer that motivated M-0521 in the first place) so the contract becomes load-bearing.
evidence: `grep -rn 'rowsForTable\|projectedRowsForTable' src/ scripts/` returns hits only in the file itself + tests. No app code consumes the helpers. The commit message d3cb3ec7 claims downstream consumers exist ('regulator-facing serializer that motivated M-0521') but they're not in this repo.
fix: Delete both helpers + their tests (-50 LoC), OR wire them into the actual download script the docstring references. The latter solidifies the contract: rowsForTable becomes a true witness, the null-return is observable, schema drift becomes detectable. The middle ground (keep dead) is the worst: future callers will revive the helpers without knowing the design rationale, and the null/[] distinction will be lost.

title: encodeExportBundle ships invalid JSON when bundle field is undefined (hand-rolled JSON.stringify(undefined) returns the JS undefined, not a string)
summary: encodeExportBundle uses template-literal concatenation: `\`{"schema_version":${JSON.stringify(bundle.schema_version)},\`` etc. When a bundle field is `undefined`, `JSON.stringify(undefined)` returns the JS value `undefined` (NOT a string). Concatenated into a template literal, it stringifies to the LITERAL 'undefined' (4 characters, unquoted) — producing invalid JSON like `{"schema_version":undefined,"user_id":...}`. JSON.parse throws on this. collectUserExportBundle today populates every field, BUT (a) encodeExportBundle is exported and callable with a hand-constructed bundle (the fallback branch at line 898 documents this use case), (b) tests construct bundles via spread/destructure where dropping a field is easy, (c) a future schema_version=2 might make optional fields legitimate, and (d) the route test mocks encodeExportBundle and never exercises the real impl with edge values. The fallback branch even calls `JSON.stringify(t.rows[r])` directly — if t.rows[r] is undefined (sparse array), it concats 'undefined'. There is no defensive check.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:910 `"fetch_error":${JSON.stringify(t.fetch_error)}` — if t.fetch_error is undefined, output is `"fetch_error":undefined` (invalid JSON). Similarly for any envelope field. JSON.stringify(b) (which the tests assert structural equivalence to) WOULD OMIT undefined-valued keys entirely — diverging from encodeExportBundle which inlines 'undefined'.
fix: Wrap each `JSON.stringify(x)` in `JSON.stringify(x) ?? 'null'` so undefined coerces to JSON null, matching how JSON.stringify handles it on the parent (key dropped, but null is the safer interpretation for an explicit field). Better: write `encodeExportBundle` as `SHARED_ENCODER.encode(JSON.stringify({ schema_version: bundle.schema_version, ... tables: tables.map(...), ... }))` reusing the cached row strings via a custom replacer. That eliminates the hand-rolled JSON entirely and reuses the standard serializer's undefined-handling.

title: Sanitize-parity CI hook does not enforce that the manifest's projected `source_table` itself appears in sanitize matrix — synthetic name allowlist hides source-table drift
summary: SANITIZE_PARITY_ALLOWLIST has entry `audit_log_for_user: PRESERVE-projection-of-audit_log`. The allowlist is consulted in `readManifestTablesForParity()` which calls a regex matching `(?:source_table|parent_table|table): "name"`. So audit_log_for_user gets added because of `table:`, AND audit_log gets added because of `source_table:`. The parity check then requires audit_log to be in sanitize matrix (it is — explicit PRESERVE). BUT: if a future migration adds a new projected entry like `kind:'projected', table:'something_for_user', source_table:'something'`, the source_table 'something' must appear in sanitize matrix. The CI hook will catch that. BUT — if a future migration RENAMES the source table (`audit_log` → `audit_events`) the manifest references would need updating; the sanitize matrix may keep the old name OR add new name. A migration that adds 'audit_events' as the new source AND removes 'audit_log' from sanitize matrix but FORGETS to update the SANITIZE_PARITY_ALLOWLIST key would leave the allowlist entry stale ('audit_log_for_user' still allowlisted as 'projection of audit_log' — but audit_log no longer exists in matrix). The parity check would scan, find that audit_log_for_user is allowlisted (skip), find that audit_events isn't in matrix (fail). But the failure message says 'audit_events -> add to sanitize matrix' WITHOUT mentioning that the allowlist entry for audit_log_for_user is now stale (its REASON 'PRESERVE-projection-of-audit_log' references a table that no longer exists in matrix). A future operator could 'fix' by adding audit_events to matrix without updating the allowlist reason — leaving a dangling provenance comment that misleads the NEXT operator. Furthermore: the allowlist key is matched by string. A typo (audit_log_for_users vs audit_log_for_user) silently fails to allowlist. Specialist type-design LOW conf-8 flagged this; apply pass did not fix.
evidence: scripts/check-gdpr-export-coverage.ts:235 (allowlist keyed by free string). scripts/check-gdpr-export-coverage.ts:543-573 (parity check). No assertion that allowlist keys correspond to actual manifest entries OR that the reason's referenced source_table is still in sanitize matrix.
fix: Add a 'allowlist consistency' pass: for each allowlist entry, verify the key appears in `readManifestTablesForParity()` output AND (for projected entries) verify the source_table from the manifest entry appears in sanitize matrix. Fail with a specific 'stale allowlist entry: <key>' message if either fails. Costs ~15 lines of additional check; closes the silent-drift regression nursery.

title: Indirect child select runs `.in(via_column, parentIds)` with 2000 UUIDs on every fetch — PostgREST URL-length cliff and pooler stress under concurrent exports
summary: The apply pass kept EXPORT_PARENT_ID_CAP=2000 (line 611) with the comment 'PostgREST has a request-line-length ceiling that hits ~2.5k elements for UUIDs'. But: the `.in(via_column, parentIds)` call serializes the 2000 UUIDs into a comma-joined query parameter (URL-encoded). For 2000 UUIDs at 36 chars + comma overhead, that's ~75KB of URL — well past the typical 8KB nginx default, past Vercel's default 16KB header limit, and past CloudFront's 8KB query-string limit. Supabase's pooler/PostgREST may accept it (the comment claims so) but ANY edge proxy in the path (Vercel Edge, CloudFront, an enterprise proxy) will 414 the request. Under bounded-concurrency 10 + 9 indirect specs that each fire a 2000-id .in(), peak concurrent in-flight URL bytes can exceed 600KB across the pool. Behavior under URL truncation by an intermediate proxy: PostgREST sees a shorter id list, returns rows for the truncated subset, the bundle silently loses data with no error and parent_id_truncated=true (set BEFORE the truncation by the parent probe size). The truncation flag isn't triggered by URL truncation — the flag is set by parent count, not by what made it to the SELECT. This is a silent partial-fetch with the truncation signal mis-attributed to a different cause. Specialist performance MED conf-8 noted serial 2-hop overhead but missed the URL cliff.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:611 EXPORT_PARENT_ID_CAP = 2000. gdpr-export.ts:1462 `.in(spec.via_column, parentIds)`. Supabase JS client serializes `.in()` as `?via_column=in.(uuid1,uuid2,...)` — URL-encoded inline. 2000 × 36 chars = 72,000 chars plus separators.
fix: Either (a) reduce EXPORT_PARENT_ID_CAP to ~200 (covers 99.99% of users and keeps URL under 10KB), with a fallback chunking strategy for 200 < parentIds.length ≤ 2000 (multiple .in() queries unioned), or (b) push the 2-hop into a Postgres RPC (`get_indirect_user_rows(table_name text, user_id uuid, ...)` that does the EXISTS subquery server-side) — single short URL, no intermediate proxy involvement. Add a regression test that asserts the .in() argument count is bounded.

title: source_truncated propagation for projected api_keys is misleading when the SQL+JS filter drop semantics diverge
summary: Apply pass added probe-with-cap+1 for projected entries (line 1351 `.limit(EXPORT_PER_TABLE_ROW_CAP + 1)`) and propagates `source_truncated: sourceArr.length > EXPORT_PER_TABLE_ROW_CAP` through to the route. But the projection function for api_keys also filters `if (row.user_id !== userId) continue` (gdpr-export.ts:406). The SQL side already filtered via `.eq(spec.user_column, userId)` — so the JS filter should be a no-op. Today it IS a no-op. But the source_truncated semantics now CONFLATE two things: (a) 'the SQL row count hit cap+1' AND (b) 'every one of those rows survived the projection'. For api_keys today these are equivalent (no projection filter beyond the redundant user_id check). For audit_log_for_user they DIVERGE: source_truncated reads cap+1 raw audit rows (post user_column SQL filter), but redactAuditLogForUser then drops rows where the subject is NEITHER actor NOR entity NOR meta-target. For a subject who's primarily a target (e.g., an allocator that admins frequently act on), the SQL filter ALONE (`.eq(user_column, userId)` — user_column='user_id') returns rows where the subject IS the actor — i.e., only rows where the subject acted. Rows where the subject is the entity/target are filtered OUT by the SQL .eq() because their user_id (actor) is the admin. So the source_truncated flag now reads ONLY the actor-subset count. A subject with 60K target-rows + 100 actor-rows gets source_truncated=false (only 100 actor-rows came back) — but the entity/target rows are silently absent from the projection's INPUT and the cap signal misses them entirely. Performance MED conf-9 specialist flagged this (audit-log SQL filter too narrow); apply pass left it; the new source_truncated flag promises completeness that the SQL fetch doesn't deliver.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:1349-1351 (.eq(spec.user_column, userId).limit(cap+1)). gdpr-export.ts:286-326 (redactAuditLogForUser accepts entity / meta-target rows but they were never fetched). USER_EXPORT_TABLES audit_log_for_user user_column = 'user_id' (line 498).
fix: The source SQL filter for audit_log_for_user must mirror the projection's OR criteria (push into PostgREST `.or('user_id.eq.<id>,and(entity_id.eq.<id>,entity_type.eq.user),metadata->>target_user_id.eq.<id>')`). Add a regression test that mocks 50K target-rows + 100 actor-rows for a subject and asserts the projection returns target rows AND source_truncated correctly reflects cap-hit on the union. Pre-fix this is a silent completeness violation; documented but unresolved.

title: fetch-error + parent_id_truncated double-signal on indirect tables produces conflicting incomplete_reasons
summary: Chains code-reviewer LOW conf-7 (#10, truncated-tail tables push truncated_at_cap=true even for fetch-error rows — fixed in apply) with a parallel issue the apply pass did NOT fix: for indirect tables, if the parent SELECT succeeds with parentIdTruncated=true and the child SELECT then errors, fetchRowsForSpec returns `{ rows: [], error: msg, parent_id_truncated: parentIdTruncated, source_truncated: false }`. The bundle then reports BOTH `fetch_error: <msg>` AND `parent_id_truncated: true` on the same payload. The route's refusal gate combines these: `failed_tables` includes the table (partial=true) AND `parent_id_truncated_tables` includes it. The audit log's `incomplete_reasons` then carries BOTH 'some tables failed to export' AND 'parent_id_cap_reached:<table>' — a misleading double-signal to forensic readers, because the parent-id cap was an UNUSED auxiliary signal: the failed fetch never used those parent ids. Worse: the rate-limit token is consumed (chain #2 finding), the user retries tomorrow expecting transient fetch failure → fixed; then sees the same combined signal because parent_id_truncated is data-dependent, not transient. The user can't disambiguate 'this is a transient error to retry' from 'this is a permanent data-volume issue requiring support'.
evidence: gdpr-export.ts:1465-1474 (indirect child fetch error returns parent_id_truncated: parentIdTruncated). The flag was set BEFORE the child select fired (line 1442) and survives the child error path. route.ts:96-114 builds incomplete_reasons unconditionally on every truthy flag — no dedup, no causality check.
fix: When `fetch_error` is set on an indirect payload, clear `parent_id_truncated` to false (or move parent_id_truncated under a discriminated `successful: true` arm of FetchRowsResult). Equivalently in the route: filter `parent_id_truncated_tables` to exclude tables that also appear in `failed_tables`. The forensic reader sees one cause per failed table.

title: Challenge — pr-test HIGH conf-9 deferred finding (happy-path account.export audit metadata) IS reachable and load-bearing, not 'covered by refusal-path test'
summary: The apply pass deferred pr-test HIGH conf-9 (No regression test for incomplete_reasons surfacing into the account.export audit-event metadata) with the rationale: 'the H-0451 test was rewritten to assert the new refusal semantics + account.export_refused audit metadata — which covers the same forensic-trail risk but on the refusal path. The happy-path metadata assertion remains a follow-up.' This rationale is INCORRECT. The two paths emit DIFFERENT audit actions (account.export_refused vs account.export) with DIFFERENT metadata shapes. The happy path's account.export audit (route.ts:246-258) writes `incomplete_reasons: incompleteReasons` — but the refusal gate ENSURES `incomplete_reasons.length === 0` is the only way to reach the happy path (lines 116-156). So in practice `incomplete_reasons` on the happy path is always []. A regression that lets a partial bundle through the gate (e.g., a refactor that moves the gate after upload, or a future PR that softens the gate to allow some truncation mode) would land WITHOUT a test that asserts incomplete_reasons is `[]` (and not undefined or missing or stale). The defer reasoning is wrong because: (a) the refusal test asserts metadata on account.export_refused, not on account.export; (b) account.export's incomplete_reasons being [] is a load-bearing invariant of the refusal-gate; (c) the test at line 462-488 asserts metadata.truncated_at_size_cap but NOT metadata.incomplete_reasons. A regression that drops the incomplete_reasons field entirely or accidentally inlines `incomplete_reasons: ['size_cap']` would leave the forensic trail incorrect with no test failure.
evidence: FIX-REPORT.md line 105-110: defer rationale. gdpr-export-route.test.ts:480-487: asserts metadata.storage_path / expires_at / table_count / total_row_count / truncated_at_size_cap — never metadata.incomplete_reasons. route.ts:256: `incomplete_reasons: incompleteReasons` IS emitted in happy-path account.export metadata.
fix: Re-classify the deferred finding from 'covered by refusal-path test' to 'NOT covered — load-bearing happy-path invariant'. Add an assertion to the existing test at line 462-488: `expect(metadata.incomplete_reasons).toEqual([]);` — pins that the happy-path emits an EMPTY array (which is the invariant the refusal gate enforces). Total cost: 1 line, removes the forensic blind spot.

title: Concurrent same-user exports race on the rate-limit token AND share the WeakMap row cache when promises interleave
summary: Chains code-reviewer LOW conf-8 (#9, TOCTOU on rate-limit) with the new WeakMap row cache. The rate-limit check consumes the token atomically, but the bundle assembly takes O(seconds) for a large user — a fast second concurrent POST that arrives BEFORE the first consumes-or-refunds can EITHER both consume tokens (Upstash returns success on both if the bucket had >1 token, which it shouldn't for 1/day BUT briefly does on first-ever exports) OR one consumes and one 429s. The token-consumed-one case is benign. The race that the WeakMap exposes: two concurrent calls to collectUserExportBundle produce TWO separate bundle objects with TWO sets of ExportTablePayload object identities. Each gets its own ROW_JSON_CACHE entries. Cross-user aliasing is impossible (different objects). BUT: a Promise.all/race situation where the first bundle's GC happens DURING the second's encodeExportBundle would WeakMap-drop the first's entries. This is safe (the second's entries are independent). The real chain risk is: an adversary who triggers 2 concurrent exports finds that ONLY ONE token is consumed (because Upstash check-then-increment isn't truly atomic under concurrent reads from the same edge region). They now have free amplification — they can run N parallel exports paying 1 token. With each export pulling 26 tables, this is a 26N-fan-out DoS amplifier against Supabase pooler. The 100MB upload cost amplifies. Code-reviewer LOW conf-8 flagged this as 'lower the rate-limit window precision (1/day is fine)' — but the apply pass didn't address it AND the new bundle-assembly cost makes the amplification more expensive per-Supabase-call.
evidence: route.ts:55-64 (checkLimit non-atomic with bundle assembly). gdpr-export.ts:656 (module-level WeakMap shared across all concurrent calls). Upstash exportLimiter: 1 token per 86400s — but read-modify-write under concurrent reads from the same region can race; mitigated by Upstash's atomic increments but vulnerable in transition windows. EXPORT_FETCH_CONCURRENCY=10 (line 595) — per export 10 in-flight queries; 2 concurrent exports → 20 in-flight, against a ~15-connection PgBouncer pool.
fix: Wrap the entire route body in a per-user mutex (Upstash lock with the same key prefix `export:${user.id}`). Lock at line 44 (immediately after auth.getUser), release in a finally block at line 270. The lock has TTL 60s (long enough for a 100MB upload, short enough to not deadlock on Vercel timeout). Token consumption then happens INSIDE the lock; concurrent calls serialize. Alternative: deduplicate via Redis SET NX on the storage key prefix.
